product_name: Pip and Posy: The New Friend
rank_rationale: #1 over #2: third-friend jealousy is the sharpest 34, 36m social wobble and Posy’s self-repair beats Little Whale’s sharing lesson on specificity, even though both clear the friendship brief.
include_rationale: Best-friend feelings run deep at nearly three, and so do the wobbles when someone new joins in. This story keeps the evening light while naming that sting and the soft way back. That gentle recovery is why it suits jealous wobbles without turning bedtime into a behaviour chat.

age_raw: Preschool picture book; publisher compares to ages 3-5 sharing titles
rank_rationale: #2 over #3: Little Whale’s snack-and-attention sharing row is fresher and more playdate-specific than the chair-territory fight in That’s (Not) Mine, which many families already know.
include_rationale: Sharing is a skill to grow into after sticky playdates, not a warning to sit through. A sunny short read lets them practise the idea with you when the house feels settled again. That warm rehearsal is why this book suits sharing rows when you want kindness more than a scolding.

rank_rationale: #3 over #4: the mine-shouting chair fight gives parents a same-day read after toy grabs, edging ahead of Sour Grape’s forgiveness arc which lands better once the row has cooled.
include_rationale: Mine fights need humour after a sticky playdate more than a talking-to. A funny short book gives you a shared joke to reset the evening without replaying the row. That light reset is why it suits mine fight nights you may want to open more than once.

rank_rationale: #4 over #5: forgiveness after a friend fallout is a harder nearly-three skill than competitive sharing, so Sour Grape ranks above Squirrels even though both model repair.
include_rationale: Holding a grudge is new at nearly three, and so is starting again. A Food Group story brings enough humour for repeat reads when the cross feeling has stuck. That kind reset is why it suits stuck cross nights without turning feelings into homework.

rank_rationale: #5: competitive nut-chase teamwork is a strong backup social-repair story but less playdate-specific than the jealousy, sharing and mine-fight picks above.
include_rationale: Competitive rows need a team ending once both children want the same prize. A lively rhyme keeps the chase fun while landing on joining in rather than who won. That joining in finish is why it suits competitive rows without dampening the fun of the race.
